text.title: generator-critic-bounded-loop Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for bounded reflection loops.

rule.statement: The loop MUST exit on the first true of three conditions: (1) critic returns `should_continue=False`, (2) score delta < epsilon for two consecutive iterations, (3) iteration count reaches the hard cap (default 3).
rule.rationale: Iteration 2 captures most of the lift, iteration 3 adds the long tail, iterations 4+ are below the noise floor. Three independent stop conditions cover correctness, efficiency, and safety.

rule.statement: The generator and critic MUST NOT share the same model AND the same temperature; either swap models, swap temperatures, or use a rubric prompt that forces a different reasoning path.
rule.rationale: Shared priors make the critic rubber-stamp the generator's mistakes; the loop adds cost without quality.

rule.statement: The critic MUST emit a structured `should_continue: bool` field; the loop MUST NOT derive continuation from a numeric score threshold.
rule.rationale: The critic has more context than a threshold rule; forcing it to emit `should_continue` explicitly keeps domain logic in the prompt instead of caller code.

rule.statement: The hard cap MUST be 3 iterations by default, with an absolute maximum of 5; values above 5 are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Unbounded reflection is the most common production cost trap; the cap defends budget when the critic logic regresses.

rule.statement: For rubric criticism (style, format, completeness), the critic MUST run on a cheap model (Haiku tier); for correctness criticism, the critic MUST run on the same tier as the generator.
rule.rationale: Rubric scoring is constrained classification; correctness checking needs the capacity that produced the answer.
